medications.drug_name: hydrocortisone 1% cream (mild topical corticosteroid) + cool compresses
medications.indication_rationale: Symptomatic relief of itchy/burning lesions; EM minor is largely managed with reassurance and symptomatic care.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply thinly twice daily to itchy areas (as per source note)

medications.drug_name: antiviral HSV suppression (e.g., aciclovir/valaciclovir) — if recurrent EM
medications.indication_rationale: Consider continuous antiviral suppression for recurrent HSV-associated EM (via GP), not needed for a single episode.
Not indicated for benign EM minor; reserve for severe/atypical disease under specialist care.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use cool compresses instead of scratching; do not pop the central blisters; monitor the mouth and eyes.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Symptom relief and early detection of any mucosal (SJS) evolution.

patient_education_points.point: These dramatic bullseye spots are erythema multiforme — a safe, self-limiting immune reaction to the recent cold-sore virus, not a live skin infection or a dangerous drug allergy. It will fade over 1–3 weeks.
Use the mild cream and cool compresses for comfort; don't pop the central blisters.
If you develop painful sores or blisters in your mouth, eyes or genitals, a high fever, or the skin starts peeling off in sheets — go to the emergency department immediately.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution; discuss antiviral suppression if HSV-associated EM recurs.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Mouth/eye/genital sores, high fever, or skin peeling — emergency care.

must_include_items: Perform a mucosal/systemic screen to exclude SJS/TEN before reassuring
Diagnose benign HSV-associated erythema multiforme minor and reassure
Symptomatic care (cool compresses, mild topical steroid); consider antiviral suppression if recurrent
errors_of_commission: Sending a clearly benign EM minor to ED (over-triage)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires a mucosal/systemic screen to exclude SJS/TEN, correct reassurance for benign EM minor, and an explicit SJS/TEN safety-net.
